messages.content: My name is Brad Burnham and I'm with Union Square Ventures.
SPEAKER_0: So let's get right to it as a private sector guy. I'd like to know how have our government institutions failed us? How are they flawed?

Small question. I think any institution is an artifact of the era in which it was created. And our institutions were created many years ago, long before we had many of the technologies that we have today, many of the forms of communication that we have today. And so I don't think they've done a great job. The government institutions have not done a great job of capitalizing on some of the technologies that we have today. So to be more specific about that. It's not just governments and most incumbents in existing industries that haven't adapted quickly. Most governments and most incumbent industries are dominated by bureaucratic hierarchies, which have existed basically for the last 150 years. And that's been the way we've organized social activity on both the public and the private sphere. We've recently begun to see the emergence of an alternative model that's based on networks. And so if you think about the way in which networks organize people, they do it in a way that's much more efficient than the current bureaucratic model. And I think the bureaucratic model on both the public and the private side is having a hard time keeping up right now.
SPEAKER_0: So, then, what compelled you to accept the invitation to come here, though?

Because I think there's an enormous opportunity to innovate in government. And I think it's going to become increasingly important. So, I invest in a lot of networks, Twitter, Tumblr, Foursquare, Etsy, things like that. These networks connect creators directly to consumers. And they eliminate a lot of the friction that existed in the earlier bureaucratic models. The governments tend to want to regulate these networks as in the same way that they regulated the original incumbents in those markets. So think taxi and limousine service and think about the way in which that regulator is challenged by the emergence of a network like Halo or Uber. And I think that it's not as simple as saying these are new models that must be allowed to run free. It's more complicated than that. There are some really interesting opportunities to regulate these new models, capitalizing on the unique characteristics of these new models. So for instance, if you think again about Uber, Uber produces an enormous amount of data about what's actually happening on the street. They know when somebody's picked up, where they're dropped off, how long it took. They have ratings on both the passengers and the drivers, and all that information is produced as a byproduct of the service that they deliver, and it's produced for nothing. Think about the difference in the way that you would regulate today. You actually hire inspectors that go out on the street. They're fairly big organizations, and they have to try and catch cab drivers behaving badly. And they don't have any of the tools that Uber provides naturally as a part of its service. We need to learn to use those tools.
SPEAKER_0: And how can we safeguard the effective use of those tools, I guess, is my first question.
How can we safeguard the – how can we ensure that the tools are used or that they're used effectively or that they're – Well, that they're used properly.
SPEAKER_0: I mean with a network like that, like Uber is – right now it's just in San Francisco. Is that right or is it all over the place? Oh, no.
It's in, I don't know, 10, 15 cities.10, 15 cities. And then Halo. There's a number of e-hailing services. Halo is in another four or five cities.

SPEAKER_0: So how can we ensure that it's being used efficiently rather? I mean is there an ability for that to be – used for, let's say someone wanted to make a certain company of drivers make them have maybe not so good of ratings all of a sudden. Are there ways that we can implement into the network some safeguards to make it run more effectively?

That's interesting. I think that that's a problem that we may one day have. It certainly isn't a problem that we have today. But that's kind of the Yelp story. I mean, how do you manage a situation where a competitor is pushing a lot of bad ratings at somebody else's restaurant? And I think what we found is it's actually fairly easy to drown that out. And it's pretty hard to actually spoof. The organic ratings because you know it always ends up coming from one IP address and and and you know then it's pretty easily deleted and stuff like that so I don't think that's the problem I think there are a bunch of other really interesting problems if you use the example of Airbnb as an example where there's a network that's creating an alternative to the existing hotel industry, where somebody who has spare capacity, a bedroom, is making that available to somebody who needs a place to sleep. Well, there's implications about exposing all of the data about where that's actually happening. Now, it's one of the ways you could regulate that market, but you have to figure out at what to what degree that data should be personally identifiable, to what degree it should be aggregated and abstracted. There's a bunch of hard problems that need to be worked through, but the important point is that there's a resource there that could allow that market to be managed in a socially responsible way much more efficiently.
SPEAKER_0: What are some of the insights that you've heard that have really grabbed you today?

I've been involved in the group that's been trying to think about how to frame questions. And we're particularly working on a problem of public participation in decision making, really, in political decision making. And the most interesting aspect of that conversation that I was a part of was trying to think through ways to create that engagement that didn't become dumbed down and yet managed to organize the questions in a way that were accessible to a larger audience. Because what happens in almost any situation, pick We were talking earlier about water, particularly in Canada, but there's a whole bunch of very technical questions about the degree to which water moves through aquifers, the degree to which certain species are dependent on certain cycles and things like that, that ordinary citizens couldn't be expected to understand. People who have a vested interest in water policy tend to invest in the scientific investigation and the scientists, frankly, to be able to debate those points. But how do you manage that if you want the wisdom of the crowds, the wisdom of the ordinary citizen to weigh in, you have to somehow take that complexity and reduce it to something that's a set of principles. And we were talking about various different ways to do that that were, I think, pretty interesting.

SPEAKER_0: You've been in the private sector for quite a while now. In the first 20 years of the private sector, especially the internet revolution, what are some of the lessons that we can learn from those first 20 years, especially since the internet revolution, that we can apply to our public institutions?

Obviously, I'm a fan, right? I believe in the Internet. I believe in the fundamental transformative ability of the Internet, and I think it's a very important thing for us as a society to embrace. But at the same time, if you wanted a lesson, we— We tend to do things in sort of boom or bust cycles in the private sector. We got very, very enthusiastic without a lot of underlying infrastructure in the late 90s. And when it became clear that there was nothing holding up that enthusiasm, that was a problem. We had a crash. And I think that, you know, I think it's. Probably also true as we think about applying the internet to the public sector, that you know we can become really enthusiastic. We can over invest and have you know problems associated with that, and then we can have disillusionment. I think the important lesson is if you can go back to the private sector: what came out of that crash was a much more sustainable commitment to the real capabilities of the internet. And I think what we have to be a little bit careful of is not getting ahead of ourselves, and yet at the same time being conscious of the possibility that we will get ahead of ourselves; and that we can't lose sight of the long-term goal just because we have, as an example, a use of this network data that causes harm to an individual inadvertently and then results in a crash in terms of the use of that kind of capacity. It takes a long time to build that back. But I think if we keep our eye on the ball long term, what we'll do is we'll realize that these are really hard problems. They're really interesting problems. It's great to have really smart people engaging in these problems, because we're talking about designing the very structure of our society going forward. And this is going to be the medium through which our society is defined going forward. So it's worth the investment.

SPEAKER_0: So it sounds like it's like let's get on the same page first and then realize we're still going to have problems. But we still need to get on that first level playing field. We have to move forward at least.

We have to move forward. I would rather move forward than get on the same page because I think what's better is to experiment with lots of different ways but run a lot of small experiments. Could be catastrophic on its own, but learn from those experiments. Rather than trying to define a perfect solution, which would take a long, protracted political process, it's like trying to define a standard in the tech world. It's just really hard. But if something emerges organically, or lots of different things emerge organically, what generally happens is one of those things grows to a point where it becomes a de facto standard. I think that's a better approach.

SPEAKER_0: I used to work with Beth when I was in law school. I was her intern when she was at the White House and stuff. And one of the things that I always was asking her about, and it's kind of appropriate now, is why can't we use some of these tools that I'm using already, like the Facebooks and the Twitters and stuff like that? And there was a little bit of that, you know, when there was like some crisis management stuff. I know Twitter was used quite frequently. But how can some of these private companies start using some of their tools and their networks for public good? I know there's always that, you know, privacy issues and the security issues that happen. Is there a way to bridge that gap?

Well, I think we have to do the heavy lifting of working through those issues. And I don't think they're simple issues. But, you know, I'll give you one that I'm always amused by. You walk around the streets of New York and you look at policemen and women on the streets of New York. I would say more than half the time they're looking at a smartphone, rather than dealing with their radio, right? I mean, at least when I'm maybe it's because I'm seeing them in their downtime and not but in any case, they're always looking at the smartphone. I read recently that they've just introduced an app that they've made available to police smartphones that actually exposes all the building department data to the smartphone so they know exactly who's in the building, you know, all the public housing authority data is now on these apps, right? Well, You could have built that as a government contract and we would have spent $30, $40, $50 million to have some systems integrator build that thing and build a special device and it wouldn't have been integrated in anything else or you allow these consumer technologies to infiltrate. Now, another area where we worry a lot about public safety is in the area of spectrum. We want to set aside spectrum for those police radios. Well, it turns out that the police are using the damn cell phone network. Maybe we should be thinking that as we look at it, the cell phone network is actually more robust than the police radio network. And we shouldn't be trying to separate out that spectrum. We should be trying to use the spectrum more efficiently and making sure that the police have some kind of privileged access to the cell network at certain times. But I think that's there's just a ton of opportunities to take consumer technologies and make them available to law enforcement, to public services. It turns out that the hard part is not the technology. I think we're already there. In the old days, we used to think about technology trickling down from NASA, you know, Tang, I think was, you know, and it came down and, you know, NASA, military, you know, ultimately civilians, right? Now what's happening is it's going civilians, military, ultimately NASA, right? And so. You know there's a reason for that, I mean there's just a much bigger market among civilians, and we now have the the base level of technology that's you know available, you know, the tools, the open source software that allow people to create these services very quickly, and there's just a ton of decentralized energy creating lots of really interesting services. So the consumer market is now moving really quickly, whereas the government market is still constrained by purchasing contract law and all sorts of other issues that make it difficult to get that technology to government services. I think we've got to break that down. I think we've got to recognize that it's moving faster on the private sector side for these consumer-facing services. And we've got to think about more ways of embracing those services. But the problem isn't technology. The problem is when you introduce these services, what are the implications? What are the implications in terms of privacy? What are the implications in terms of security of those networks, consumer safety? There's a whole bunch of problems that we have to think through. But they're all, unfortunately, they're not simple technical problems in which, you know, I just got to make this chip go 10 times faster. That's a fairly constrained problem. They're moral problems. You know, what's the right balance between an individual's right to privacy and the government's right to know? You know, those are not simple questions.
SPEAKER_0: And those become political questions, too, at a certain point, which always makes everything much more difficult.
Right.

SPEAKER_0: So my last question is: so this is just an experiment here today. We can call this experiment one here at the summit. What would you think Experiment 2 should look like? We're all together again. What are the types of questions or what are the types of outputs that you would like to see in the next time we do something like this?

Well, I don't know. I think it probably depends a little bit on the audience that you gather. I feel if I had a criticism of where we were today, there was a lot of conversation, at least in my group, that was very thoughtful, but very abstract. And I couldn't wrestle it down to the ground to understand immediately what to do with it. One of the great things about working in the private sector where I work is there's this concept of a hackathon where people actually spend 24 hours and and come out with something that they can show um there's something great about that if there were a way to introduce i know there's some some of that working its way through here but i you know i'm not close enough to understand exactly how that's happening but i would i would say figuring out a way to deliver something more tangible um and one One way one could think about that, for instance, I mean, obviously governments have been experimenting with opening up APIs and opening up government data and letting people hack on that data and come up with applications. And there have been some neat examples of things that have been done. But maybe incorporating something like that so that there was a sense of more tangible output would be fun. I would certainly agree with that.

Thank you, Magnus. Well, I have to say I'm very intimidated having watched the President stand up here with no notes and talk for 15 minutes or 20 minutes about all of the things that I care about, and so I am really kind of panicked at the thought of trying to follow that. I'm going to start by talking a little bit about Union Square Ventures and what we invest in. That's where I work. First, in doing that, my purpose is to, first of all, give you a sense of where we're coming from. I had a professor once who was teaching political science, and he said, you know, the important thing is where you stand depends on where you sit. So I'm going to tell you where I sit, and that will give you a little sense of why I'm approaching the problems that I'm going to talk about the way I am, and also a way of filtering my biases, because I do have biases. So we are information technology investors. We don't do nanotech or biotech or material science or energy or anything like that. We do information technology. And within that, we invest exclusively in the applications layer of the internet. We don't do chips, routers, infrastructure, telecom. We do the services that ride on top of all of that infrastructure. And within that, we invest in networks. So we don't invest in e-commerce, for instance. We invest in networks. And those networks bring together the creators of a service and the consumers of that service. And we do that because we think these networks are the most powerful and fundamentally transformative forces that we see today in the global economy. And why is that? Why do these networks have this ability? Well, they are actually bringing some really amazing economics to problems as diverse as housing, healthcare, finance. So how does that happen? Well, if you think about it, By connecting a creator and a consumer directly, you're cutting out what was the model for producing economic value for about 200 years. Interestingly, not 2,000 years, 200 years. The bureaucratic hierarchy, the organization, the firm, has only been a model for producing economic value for about 200 years, but that's the model that we know. But if you are, as an example, Lending Club, which is one of our portfolio companies, you connect a borrower directly with a lender over an information system. Well, think about the way that that was done in the past. There was a bank that took in deposits from people and then turned around and had a process for lending that deposit. That was a fairly high-cost process. Well, how is it possible that this happens on the internet? It happens because there's a layered architecture. The internet separates the communications layer, all of the you know, the problems of getting a bit from point A to point B from the applications layer. And, you know, if anybody's studying this, there's a whole sophisticated sort of seven-layer model for how it actually does that. That's kind of an accident. That was a bunch of engineers who designed it in that way. But the result of that is that you as an entrepreneur can reach a global audience without paying for any of that infrastructure. You know, historically, if I wanted to provide a service to somebody in Bangladesh, I basically had to construct a path to get my service from here to Bangladesh. Today, everybody assumes that that infrastructure is there. So I've separated the communications layer. The other piece of it is that that communications layer is open and permissionless. The president talked about this, talked about the importance of openness. The fact that anyone in this room can create a service and distribute it on the internet without asking a telecommunications company to distribute it for them, without asking a bank to package and distribute their products. They can create a product and distribute it directly to the web. What that's doing, if you think about it, is it's making it possible for probably the biggest explosion of creativity in the last, well, certainly in the last couple hundred years. And we're seeing that on the internet today. We're seeing fabulous efficiencies in the creation of new companies. Probably 100 times faster than Foursquare got to 100,000 users on $25,000, that Tumblr was in 100 countries when they had 12 people. That wasn't possible 15 years ago. It is possible today. So it's that creativity that makes possible. It's that layered architecture, the free distribution, the low cost of creating new companies, the existence of open source software, the permissionless model of the internet that's making for this explosion and making for this opportunity in Iceland, but also for every entrepreneur all over the world. The way these services produce these economics, just to go down that, just to describe that in a little bit more detail, if you think of a company like Airbnb, for instance, Airbnb doesn't actually provide a hotel service. It connects somebody who has a spare bedroom with somebody who has an interest in traveling or staying in Iceland. If you think of Halo or Uber, these are both e-hailing services for the transportation industry. It's no longer a a dispatcher who you call to say, I need a cab in front of the Hilton to take me to Harpa, you're actually connecting directly with the driver. If you're using a ride-sharing service and somebody's traveling from Dusseldorf to Hamburg, you can go to carpooling and you can take advantage of that ride. Well, what's actually happening there? We're doing a much better job as a society of using existing resources. That person is going to travel from Dusseldorf to Hamburg anyway, right? If you put two people in that car, you eliminate the need for another form of transportation, right? If you can house people in spare bedrooms, you're making a much better use of that resource. We're improving the access to creativity on the Internet. We're also improving the use of assets. And it's that that sort of unlocks the economics. So this is really good for everybody, except for the high-cost bureaucratic hierarchies that provide the services today, except for the incumbents. Well, that's okay. This is part of the natural creative destruction of capitalism. We're going to move through and constantly evolve towards a more efficient model. That's what capitalism does. Unfortunately, the way the political process works, at least in the United States, is that the incumbents have an outsized influence in the formation of policy and the way regulations are created. Well, in the United States, that happens as a result of the way we finance campaigns and the fact that the incumbents have existing cash flows. The new startup companies tend not even to be politically aware, much less have the cash flows to participate in that game. And so what we see as a result is we see a series of proposals from the incumbents that would constrain the emergence of these new systems, these new models. When the city of New York received, debated, the city council in New York debated a law against temporary hotels a year and a half ago, nobody from the hotel industry lobby that proposed the law mentioned Airbnb. They did mention, you know, unscrupulous landlords who were converting flop houses into SROs and renting them out to unsavory people. And they came up with lots of reasons why this was a bad idea. And the city council actually had never heard of Airbnb, so they had no idea. That the language that was being proposed would actually constrain the emergence of this new model. But the hotel industry knew that. The hotel industry actually wrote the language and suggested it to the city council. So we have a law in the city of New York that outlaws temporary hotels, and we've begun suing, not suing, we've begun fining Airbnb hosts for hosting guests in their spare bedrooms. So this is going to be a problem. And it's going to be a problem because we are constraining the natural evolution of the economy. And if you think about the remarks about anti-fragile and the ability to create an environment which benefits from randomness, which benefits as it's exposed to randomness, I think what you really want is one that does that in a graceful way. You could argue that, and the President did argue, that Iceland benefited from the financial collapse. I think that's true. In New York City, we had the same financial collapse, and the startup community in New York benefited greatly from the financial collapse. Wouldn't it be better if we could limit the amount of damage? If we could, instead of waiting for a very big pent-up problem to crash and then rebuild from that, wouldn't it be better to have lots of small crashes that were spread across the economy, affected nobody as dramatically as the financial collapse affected us? Well, that is what you're looking for in an anti-fragile system. Unfortunately, to the degree that the incumbents are able to use policy and regulation to resist the emergence of these new hyper-efficient models, The result of that is not going to be that the hyper-efficient models don't ultimately win because it's kind of like grass growing up through the pavement. It will ultimately win, right? The result of that is going to be that the collapse is going to be much more disruptive, right? It's not going to be small. It's not going to be incremental. It's not going to be graceful. It's going to be much more disruptive. So what can you as members of the startup community do to help, you know, move through this part of our sort of natural economic evolution? Well, the first thing I think we have to do is may seem like a little bit of a contradiction. The first thing I think we have to do is recognize that there is a role for the government in the economy. And there is a role for regulation. A lot of people who are sort of internet purists or internet libertarians or internet utopians, cyber utopians, believe that we just want to let the internet run and all of these problems will work out. You know, there are going to be problems with there being unscrupulous landlords on Airbnb. There are nasty drivers on Uber. There are going to be problems in this economy. And so we need to recognize that these problems exist. We need to recognize that. Yes, you as an entrepreneur can reach a customer in Bangladesh, but a unscrupulous hacker in Russia can also steal money from your mother across the globe. So we have to recognize that there is a role. If we recognize that, we can begin a dialogue with how to do it. The second thing we need to do is recognize that It's going to be the natural inclination of regulators and politicians to try and manage the economy in a way that they understand. And they don't completely understand the internet. Many of the people that run the policy process in the United States are still having secretaries print out their emails. So this is a new world for them. So we need to help educate them on how to work with this. You know, the second important piece. Recognize that there is a role and then think about how that role can be sort of reinvented to take advantage of this new network as opposed to try and fight against it. So I'm going to throw out a notion that I call Regulation 2.0. And, you know, in thinking about that, the key is: Instead of a policymaker or a regulator thinking of the internet as the problem, they should be thinking of the internet as the solution. So let me give you an example in the area of regulating transportation. Today, in the city of New York, the way we regulate the taxi industry is we put inspectors on the street in cars, usually unmarked cars, and they flag. They flag taxi drivers who are doing things that they don't think are legal, running around with passengers with their off-duty sign on and things like that. Then they flag them and they give them a ticket. That is a very high-cost model. If you think of what an Uber or Lyft does, because it's a network that connects a driver directly with a passenger, there is an enormous amount of information in that network. You know where the ride was picked up, where the passenger was picked up. You know where the passenger went. You know exactly how they traveled. You know how long it took them to travel that route. You know exactly how much they paid. The driver rates the passenger, and the passenger rates the driver. That's an enormous amount of information that a regulator has never seen before in their lives. If the regulators recognize that and are willing to work with that network, then there's a tremendous opportunity. The problem is that we are actually treating, the regulators are still treating these networks as the problem, not the solution. And therefore, they're trying to put them in a box. They're trying to say, 'okay', that the network that links the driver and the passenger or that links the borrower and the lender is behaving as a bank or an educational institute or a hotel, and they're trying to regulate them with the same outdated regulations that they regulate the existing model. And as a result, the networks are very reluctant to share data with the regulators because if you're going to use the data that I give you to put me out of business, I'm not going to give you the data. So what we need to do is we need to say, okay, well, wait a minute. What if we understand that these are actually intermediaries and not? We're not actually driving anybody. We're not actually putting anybody up. We're not actually lending money. We're just facilitating that transaction between two willing parties. And then the regulator recognizes, okay, I've got to regulate the edge of that network, not the middle. And if I give that network some protection from liability, then I can expect them to share data with me, and that will give me the tool to efficiently manage the edge of that network. So that's a new model of regulation. So why do we need to think about this? We need to think about this because I think startups are going to have to engage in the political process. We have historically, as venture capitalists and startups, been kind of allergic to the political process. We haven't wanted to be involved. And for a long time, we haven't had to be involved. In the era where we were doing switches and routers and chips, those things are making some existing capability 10 times faster. That's completely apolitical. Nobody really cares. But when you move up into the applications layer and you challenge an incumbent in the area of financial services, people care. And you immediately have to become politically engaged. So we are going to have to become politically engaged. You know, it's interesting. One of the criticisms of our community that I think is deserved is that we have demonstrated that we can stop things. You know, when we really put our back up against the wall when there was really dramatic overreaching legislation in the US coming down the pike, the internet community kind of rose up and said no. And everybody was completely shocked when that happened, and it was very effective. But we haven't demonstrated that we can advocate for things. It's a much harder problem. If you think about it, you could argue that the open internet was a key factor in the emergence of the Arab Spring, in changing those societies across the Middle East. Demonstrated that it can build a new government, that it can build a new society, right? So this is the challenge that I think we have for ourselves is to figure out how to take these tools and make them productive as opposed to just using the tools to manage, you know, sort of to defend our existing freedom to innovate. So I'll just close by coming back to Iceland. Again, the president mentioned that maybe there is a role for Iceland in this. I think there is. It's why I've been here as much as I have been over the last couple of years. Actually, the last year, as I think about it. The first time I ever came was for this event last year. And I've been back a number of times since, talking to a full range of people in the community here about the role Iceland could play. I realize that in saying there's a special opportunity for Iceland, there is a sort of, it reminds some people of, well, we thought that in the financial Look how that turned out. And so it's a very tricky thing to suggest that there's a special role. But I do think there is one. I think that there is a natural, for all of the reasons people have already talked about today, this is a naturally entrepreneurial society. There's a significant opportunity to build an IT-related business. You can reach a global market from this isolated community at the edge of the world. And I think there is, there's all the advantages of data centers and energy and everything else. So there is that, right? But it's also a homogeneous population with a tractable political process which could push the model for policymaking and regulation incrementally in a direction that could build a global brand for Iceland. It could say that Iceland is thinking about a a model of regulation that works with the internet as opposed to against the internet. That it's thinking about the way users'rights in their data exist in the cloud or in cyberspace. There's a lot of confusion about that in the US and in Western Europe, but there's an opportunity for Iceland to begin to define that. These are hard problems. They're not simple. There are some principles, I think, that could be established, but I think it will take some work for Iceland to define where they want to land on the spectrum. If you push the agenda too far out, then you end up looking like you're just trying to get, you know, the Internet's about. Libertarianism and sort of free stuff and there is no sort of economic development message. I don't think that's the answer. I think you want to push it in a direction that says there's a huge opportunity for innovation and economic development on this platform. Let's see what constraints we need to release in order to be able to unleash that economic development. It's going to be tricky politically in the United States. One of the things that's interesting is that This kind of policy, this net-friendly policy, isn't about left or right. I mean, there's a whole bunch of issues that I end up working with both the left and the right on in the United States. It's really about future and past. And I think the opportunity for the political process here in Iceland, like the process in the US, is for one of the parties to seize that vision of the future and say, we want to define that vision of the future. And I think what will happen is that they will end up bringing with them the young people, the people who grew up on the internet, who grew up inside of Facebook, who recognize the value of it, and that will be a very strong political base. But it will be tricky. So that's the thoughts that I wanted to share. That's it. Thank you.

SPEAKER_5: So William III told the Dutch when Louis XIV was coming for them once that they had enough shipping in their harbors. They could put all of Holland on boats and go and start another Holland in the South Seas. And my friends, whose Yugoslavia was destroyed in the early 90s, many of them who considered themselves Yugoslav citizens of a place that didn't exist anymore wanted to make cyber you and have continuity that way. So facing this as a kind of national resiliency, it seemed to you like a credible prospect. Stalin would say, yeah, but socialism is where the Red Army stands. And I think meatspace still has some priority in that sense. But that ability to create identity or to run a national ratings system to attach the money to the behavior patterns. Is this why the Chinese Bitcoin mining bothers you, or is it just that if they had 51%, they could rewrite transaction history? It's history, not just transaction history, which is at stake in Brad's example, right? This is national identity and the flow of history, and the question of who gets to rewrite that. When I think about those public records becoming evidence, When I think about the long flow of the land law from the 11th century version that I teach in medieval English legal history in which we don't have any central records and we're never going to have them. messages.content: SPEAKER_2: Well, so I just want to come back to this rhetoric, which is not in this room, but rhetoric. So one thing that the So Jamie made this link to the credit issue. We heard the governance issue, which is definitely one of the biggest problems, I think, in the cooperative world is governance. And I think this would be central. I'd like to hear from each of you a bit on that, like how you make that link. How would you see that link? How can your insight help this community? And then I can make a comment.

So this community is thinking about platforms and co-ops. And so I really like the focus on the metagame, and I think that we've talked a lot about governance. We've talked about designing it or allowing it to emerge through market mechanisms on these platforms with a new foundation. I'll make it very concrete to make it a little bit easier. We have a problem with Uber. The problem with Uber is that it's based on an equity model. And the most recent valuation of Uber was about $60, $70 billion. And that creates an expectation of earnings in a few years of about $10 billion. That's a lot more than they have today. Because in order to deliver a return to their equity investors, they have to find a way to either expand geographically or expand vertically into multiple industries, or increase their take rate. So if you're on that platform, if you're a driver or a passenger on that platform, it means that you can expect that more and more of the value created in that platform is going to go to the equity shareholders over time. And that means it's coming out of your pocket. I think there is an opportunity. If you think about what Uber actually does, it coordinates the activity of a driver and a passenger. They had some brilliant innovations like using the GPS and the devices, using a map to give you confidence that the car was actually coming to you. All of these things were really wonderful innovations that made a huge difference in reputation systems, made a huge difference in giving you the confidence to use the network. It hasn't changed much since those original innovations. And so you can imagine that that platform could be reduced to a protocol. And if it is reduced to a protocol, and that protocol uses a token to incent the economic behavior, to get a driver to show up, to give passengers the confidence that a driver will show up, then what you can imagine, the difference is that instead of company that's trying to create you know monopoly profits for the longest period of time and then return those to shareholders; then if the token is mediating the behavior on the network then in the driver's cash register in the passenger's wallet as that token as more people come to the network that token appreciates, the participants in the network will participate in the value that they're creating in that network it's a fundamentally different model and I think it's one that is really not too far off and we're going to begin to see it more and more on all of these platforms in the in the near future; um yeah that's a that's a great example; um I also want to look at the question the other way around.

The Research Works Act played out in a very similar way here in Washington. It's original sponsors understood it as the elimination of a government mandate that forced researchers to embrace a specific business model. The academic publishers who sponsored the bill knew very well that it would slow competition from open access journals. I could go on and on. Telecom companies asking regulators to impose new burdens on Skype, or toy manufacturers asking regulators to force Etsy sellers who make hand-carved wooden toys to be subjected to a rigorous certification process that only makes sense for large manufacturers. The point is that we have to step back and see these policy proposals as the inevitable byproduct of the transformation of our economy. We have to see them as part of the competition between the bureaucratic hierarchical model for the creation of economic value and the new emergent economics of networks. We need to be smart enough to recognize that it's not consumers asking for these regulatory or legal restrictions, it's the incumbents. We need to defend the freedom to innovate because it is critical to the health of our economy. Our economy is today one of the most innovative and friendly innovation of friendly economies in the world. Until recently, no one investing in or creating a business on the internet would have considered building their business anywhere else. But the recent enforcement actions against Mega Upload and JotForm have forced entrepreneurs and investors in internet services to rethink where their businesses are based. I don't know the particulars of the JotForm case, and it is very hard to defend the behavior of the Mega Upload founders, but it is also impossible to ignore the fact that the site takedowns made it impossible for hundreds of thousands of people to get to completely legitimate content that they had stored on those sites. In the weeks that followed those takedowns, every one of our portfolio companies had to reconsider where their users'Data is stored. All of them are now wondering if they should be moving to a domain name server outside this country. The Internet is a global network. There are countries out there that recognize the opportunity to create an internet enterprise zone. They are working to establish a policy framework that protects users'data and, more broadly, the freedom to innovate. I cannot tell you when or even if data and the good systems administration jobs that go with it will move offshore, but I can tell you that that conversation has already started. But networks are not just critical to our economy. They are critical to a vital civil society. It is pretty clear that we will not have the ability to continue to live in the manner to which we've become accustomed. We are very likely not going to be able to support things we value, like the arts and social services, in the way that we'd like. We're going to have to learn to do more with less. Networks can play a role here, too. Kickstarter, a crowdfunding network launched only a few years ago, will provide more support for the arts this year than the National Endowment. In the UK, lawmakers are just as frustrated as we are that banks are still not lending to small businesses. But they have a regulatory framework that allowed Funding Circle, a peer-to-peer network of lenders and borrowers, to flourish. Lawmakers have now begun encouraging their constituents not just to shop local and eat local, but to lend local. By asking them to lend local, they have created a brand new source of working capital for small businesses, and an emotional and financial return for lenders. There are many, many other examples of networks making a difference in civil society, from mapping slums in Kenya, to getting at-risk kids in New York to take better care of their health, to empowering mothers in Boston to take on gang violence. If we defend the freedom to innovate, we will find lots of ways to efficiently deliver important social benefits. Of course, we should expect the same resistance from the incumbent bureaucracies in the public sector who regard these services as their turf. Innovation depends on keeping the cost of innovation down, making sure that financing is available, and making sure that markets are accessible. It does not depend on R&D grants or targeted industrial policy. So the next time so the next time you see a piece of legislation that has an impact on an open internet, software or business method patents, copyright enforcement, free and fair competition, open government, or cybersecurity, I urge you to see it through the lens of the competition between incumbent industrial hierarchies and emergent networks. Consider who is sponsoring the legislation. Does it really protect consumers? Or does it protect the business models and cost structures of the incumbents? I recently heard a woman from the Occupy movement say the most poignant thing. She said, no one is coming for us. Her generation does not expect the government to be there when they need it. Nor do they think that incumbent industrial hierarchies are structured or motivated to solve the challenges that they expect to face. Remarkably, she was not depressed, defeated, or bitter. She was determined. The kids who grew up inside AOL chat rooms and came of age on Facebook have an intuitive understanding of the power of networks that our generation will never have. They are not asking us to fix the problems we have left them with. They are asking us not to get in their way as they try and dig themselves out. Think we owe them that. Thank you.

SPEAKER_5: So, in addition to practicing law at price zero and making it up on volume, part of our process is that we also, because we work for free, we work very fast. And we try and be extremely responsive, unlike certain other kinds of lawyers who need you to come back and pay for more clarifications for everything you say. It doesn't do us any good, so we try and say it right the first time, and then they won't come back, which at price zero is the correct answer. Therefore, I like to give answers to questions immediately, and I like them to be complete and absolute, and to leave no space. And as it happens, as I say, so I should read, because bank executive titles are the things you should read off. Pieces of paper, I'm going to get this wrong, Keith Agasson is senior vice president and associate general counsel for global operations and what is the other part before we get to into our technology and global operations and global intellectual property for Bank of America, so you can understand why the title is so long because the bank is so big. Also also an alumnus of Columbia Law School, which in this room is the most important point, formerly of what was Robeson Gray, before it was Robeson Gray, when it was Fish and Neve, which in my childhood, when I was a kid, IBM Lawyer was the only law firm I knew that could bill more intensively to IBM than Cravath. So Keith comes from places where money is, like Columbia Law School and Fish and Neve and Bank of America. And what he thinks about is these questions of global IP and compliance. And your question is about to be answered by the experts. So, Keith, I turn it over to you.

SPEAKER_3: Sure. Thanks. So, you know, I think what's interesting, particularly for people that are not, you know, in banking day in, day out, is that there's a dichotomy. That I think you see in the financial services space, which, again, if you're not there, may seem a little bit like a bipolar situation. On the one hand, you have every day, as Chris mentioned, there's new announcements about new proof of concepts that are being tried by the banks, a lot of experimentation, whether it be in payments, settling trades, letters of credit, trade finance. The regulators themselves are out there touting how great this could be. Yesterday, the OCC announced that they're going to have a new division focused on FinTech. CFPB has lauded blockchain and the opportunities there to faster payments, lower fees, more transparency for consumers. And so if you just look at that, you say, hey, this is great. The regulators are all behind this. They want this to happen. Smooth sailing. I think where the dichotomy comes in, there's always that tension, right? There's a tension between wanting the innovation, wanting what's good for consumers, the public financial system, with the regulators'need to ensure kind of the safety and soundness of the financial system. And so you take all the positives and all the accolades, and if you just look at that, you assume regulations are not a problem. I think what's instructive, there was a speech two weeks ago by one of the Federal Reserve governors, Bernanke, and she said some interesting things. She talked about innovation and what blockchain could do to revolutionize the industry. It could make faster payments, you could lower fees, more transparency. But then she went on and spent most of her speech talking about the need to ensure the safety and soundness. She talked about long-established guardrails, which I think is translating into regulation. She talked about the need for technology firms and banks to ensure that they're managing risk appropriately. Again, translate that to the regulations. What's interesting is that she went on, she kind of went beyond. So normally, it's kind of safety and soundness is kind of the core of financial regulation. And she talked about, you know, the question with blockchain will be, does it increase the risk or decrease the risk compared to what we have today, which is normal. She went on then and talked about two additional risks, which kind of new take on this. So not only does it increase or decrease risk, but does it increase or decrease the transparency of the risk? And who bears that risk between the banks, technology providers, and end users? So I think kind of the take-home message in the regulation question is that while there's certainly encouragement, I think at the end of the day, safety and soundness is what the regulators That's their job. That's their responsibility. And so whatever happens with blockchain, however it develops in the financial space, I think you have to keep an eye on what the regulations require.

SPEAKER_5: So what has happened so far, if I can summarize this before I bring another big gun onto the table. What has happened so far as we've established, I hope, is there is another paradigm of how software works in the world coming into existence. That paradigm has to do with self-authenticating information meant to be shared. Whether shared publicly or shared among a closed group of partners, the point about this information is that at one and the same time it requires and enables trust. And because it both requires trust and enables trust, it creates both enormous efficiencies in trust and also great difficulties in understanding where it should be used and what the risks are of using it. But this approach to making software, that is to say we have a way of authenticating information on its own face that can be shared, is going to become as important to the way software works in the world as another idea, let's call it relational databases, began to be in the 1970s. We have ways of storing and sharing information, but they're extremely different now. And the technical design paradigm is now also a set of social consequences, which we don't quite understand. And there are a lot of use cases, but exactly what are they? What Keith has just said at the beginning of his contributions to this, as far as I am concerned, is now what we really have to recognize is that because this comes from money and is about money, The initial forms of skepticism have to do with regulatory questions about whether you're about to blow out the economy or improve the economy. Are we increasing productivity or are we creating a DAO that could turn out to be a big problem? Back in the early 1990s when I was working to free encryption, which everybody else knew was a really bad idea, and therefore to create electronic commerce, which everybody knew would be a really good idea, but they weren't willing to have encryption in order to get it. God knows what would have happened if they hadn't gotten encryption over their wishes. But back when I was trying to work on this problem, one of the people that I worked with was a cryptographer in California named Hal Finney, who was very important to the creation of version one of PGP. And if I had to tell you who I most suspect of being any particular part of Satoshi Nakamoto, it would be the late Hal Finney. And the evidence I would offer you is that the first transaction of Bitcoin, Hal Finney was a counterpart. So the records say that, unless somebody has adjusted history in order to come over the prehistory of Satoshi Nakamoto. The point about this, of course, is that it was money from the beginning and it was; I know that when Chris says anarchist in that tone of voice, he's not trying to offend my clients. But let's face it, it was anarchy and money from the beginning, and that means there's going to be a radical degree of doubt about all sorts of things, which I thought Chris has very well expressed, and which anybody who works for a major money center bank doesn't want to have to get close to. And we have Columbia alums all over, including the former New York State Banking Commissioner who first had to think about Bitcoin. We all know that everybody is going to be thinking about this shortly. In fact, I was sitting here next to my colleague, Conrad Johnson, and wondering whether anybody was going to have the heart to go and tell the securities teachers upstairs that they have no idea how the stock market is going to work in six years. We are going to change all this and it's going to be a very complicated business and there's going to be a lot of skepticism on the part of regulators who both do want it to work because it could be so wonderful and are afraid that it's going to blow fuses they don't understand. Chris?

SPEAKER_2: I just wanted to add, sort of following up on the open governance aspect and why it's so fundamentally important that we do this in open governance. And that is because we actually are trying to engage the regulators in this process as well. As we evolve and develop the software in the Hyperledger community, certainly we want to engage and have the regulators and the governments and so forth all be part of this whole conversation.

SPEAKER_5: Which means it has to be open because otherwise we could brief the regulators, but then we'd have to kill them, right? Or we'd have to send Goldman Sachs alums to be the regulators because they would be the only people we could trust. That's another part of this problem. So, yeah, please.

SPEAKER_3: Could I jump in that? The open governance thing, and just to kind of give kind of, again, the bank perspective. And I think you've got to find the right balance and tension, right? Because, you know, again, from the regulator's perspective, if you're dealing with financial infrastructure, that's what you're talking about, they want to know that in the event of a crisis, right, there's somebody accountable, someone that is on the hook to get that back up and running. And so you look at some of the existing regulations out there. So the SEC, for example, has some recovery time objectives that came out shortly after September 11th, and depending on how big of a market player you are, if you take 5% of any market, any securities, any equity market, there's a two-hour recovery time objective. The latest cyber rules that just came out last week, the proposed rules, two-hour recovery time objective. If you have completely open governance, you'll never be able to achieve that.

SPEAKER_2: I'm talking about the governance of creating the software, not But to that point, yes.

SPEAKER_3: If there's a problem in the software, you've got a governance to fix it.

SPEAKER_5: So if that exchange gets put in our larger sense, what we're now saying is we know we have to have openness in the way we construct this technology. It is not mature technology. We're going to have to change it as we use it in order to deal with desperately complicated problems that most of the people responsible for taking political heat for will not know how to solve. And nobody wants to hear that the way this is done is on a Debian mailing list. And they're right, except, of course, that we're still anarchists and we actually still do believe that freedom is important to all of this somehow. So the good news is that the people who are going to cause all of this to fall into place are neither anarchists nor compliance types. They're investors, and they want to do other things with this, and they are going to define in some fundamental sense how a lot of this happens because their investments are going to be about the sequencing with which it happens. When I am teaching law students or young lawyers about strategy, I explain to them that good strategy knows what's going to happen next, but that really good strategy knows in what order it is.will happen, because until you know in what order things will happen, you are not actually making strategy. And in the technical economy of innovation in the United States, and therefore the world, the people who determine the sequence are known to others as the Viet Cong, and they make decisions for their own reasons. Nowadays, as you probably know, when a telecommunications company wishes to innovate, having exported all its innovative people, it goes and buys something for $85 billion and hopes that there is some innovation in there. This is not always how it was. When we had a telecommunications monopoly in the United States, it did a lot of innovative thinking, to which we owe stuff like Unix and C, and a whole bunch of innovation we are still living off, which occurred at Bell Labs, where innovation and telecommunications were not absolutely incompatible. Determine an awful lot about the future of blockchain technology starts as that rarity of current American life, innovation in telecoms. Brad was the one who began to realize that there was stuff at Bell Labs that could make money, which turned out not to be what Bell Labs as a whole was very good at doing. And he and Fred Wilson made Union Square Ventures, which could make money out of Twitter, for example, which I think Mr. Dorsey is going to have a hard time doing, and has been early in an awful lot of the future we presently have, whether it's Twitter or Foursquare or, for that matter, Coinbase. Union Square Ventures was there early. I guess it must have gotten out good. Isn't that the whole point? So, tell us from your point of view, where are the opportunities now for investment-driven innovation to clarify all of this and make some things happen? Where do you think they are, and what do you think it's going to be?

Thank you. So I think most of the conversation so far has been about the opportunity to use this technology to improve the efficiency of existing organizations and existing business processes. And I think that's a very real opportunity.lunch sort of by accident with a senior executive at a major money-sitter bank who was very, very actively involved in a blockchain implementation. And he pointed out something really interesting to me. He said that normally in the business cycle, the financial services industry would be consolidating right now very quickly. And they would be doing that because of the additional capital requirements and regulatory overhead. It's becoming more and more difficult. To extract margins from proprietary trading and other places that had supported their cost structure and so there's a lot of pressure on costs now and a lot of the cost in these in these businesses is in their back office, in their administration, in their trade settlement, and And they're looking at those back offices and they're saying, 'Gosh, if we could merge with one of our competitors, we could consolidate that back office.'But Elizabeth Warren is not going to let us merge with one of our competitors. So how can we get the efficiencies of a consolidated back office without consolidating the front office? And the answer turns out to be it has to be a shared data set, a shared database. And so I don't know if I just lost my mic, but I got it. I'm still here, still here? Okay. So anyway, I thought that was a very interesting observation. I think that's a very real opportunity. I think there will be a lot of value created or a lot of efficiencies created and therefore margins created in existing businesses by sharing that database across multiple organizations, whether it's a supply chain or a settlement operation. I'm actually personally interested in a more disruptive opportunity, one that changes the structure of markets. And I'd go back to sort of put this in context to the world that existed before the internet. Prior to the internet, The media industry was defined by distribution. You had a television station, a radio station, a newspaper. Those distribution channels controlled access to consumers and therefore controlled what the consumers consumed. At some point, we all kind of accidentally agreed to communicate with each other directly over a protocol called TCP/IP. And all of a sudden, it was possible for anyone to get to anyone else in the world. And those distribution bottlenecks went away overnight. And there's been a fundamental disruption in the media industry and the telecom industry. People in this room probably remember at some point paying for long distance calls. Does anybody remember that? Has anybody done that recently? Prisoners. Prisoners are the only ones actually paying for long distance calls today. So what happened there is we moved from a world where telecommunications was controlled by a corporation and and the switching was done through a central switch, so in order to connect to somebody across the world you had to go through a central switch who could extract rents in that process. We moved to a world where we said, well, wait a minute, that's just basically a That can be reduced to a protocol. We can just define how we're going to exchange messages with each other. And we can load that protocol on every server. And that protocol was called TCP/ IP. And we loaded that on every server. And all of a sudden, anybody who sent a message from one of those servers to the next, the packet knew where to go. And it didn't need a central switch. And so that phenomenon is actually what's happening with the blockchain. You know, if we think about what's happening in the web today, we're in another era of consolidation. It actually looks to us as investors a lot like the world looked in the mid-'90s when the first question that you had to ask any potential startup was, how are you going to compete with Microsoft? Today, you have to ask, how are you going to compete with Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon? And the acronym is now GAFA, if you want to shorten that. So how are you going to compete with GAFA? And it's a very real question, because if you think about what was the last major consumer web service of scale to get to global scale, the answer is Snapchat. Snapchat is now over five years old. There has not been another consumer web service of scale since then. There's a reason for that. There's a reason Twitter today just shut down Vine, their video service. There's a reason why companies can't compete with Facebook Live. And it's called a network effect. And the network effect that creates the marker power for Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon is a result of having the data about all of their consumers in a single place. So the database replaces the central switch that the telecommunications industry had. And so now, the profound thing that's happening with the blockchain is the possibility of creating systems where that data is distributed amongst multiple people and no longer concentrated in a single vendor. I'll just leave it at that for a moment and say I think that there will be efficiencies created by private blockchains which will allow existing businesses to become more and more efficient. I think that's great. The more profound change is going to come as public blockchains allow for the disaggregation of market power and the reemergence, or what Tim Berners-Lee calls the re-decentralization of the web.

SPEAKER_5: Okay, so let's follow that just one more step in the telecommunications sector. FCC is now going to tell the telecoms that they have to get opt-in for the collection of user behavior data. And what I think you just said was, now, if we were just a little further along in the maturation of the technologies I want to push, FCC might be in a position to consider another regulatory alternative, which is you can collect that user data only if you're putting it in something which has the anti-competitive and re-decentralizing effect that you're talking about. That is to say, user behavior data can be authenticated information that only exists in a shareable form. You want to get real anarchist about this with me and imagine that that's actually the goal? Or do you want to stop a little bit short of that process in which we, oh, I hate to use this word, socialize all that behavior data in a trustworthy way?

So I think you're giving Tom Wheeler too much credit. I think he's a very good administrator. I think he's done a very good job of managing a whole bunch of very complex problems, including net neutrality, and I'm very pleased to have him do what he has done. That doing what he's done here by requiring the telecommunications industry to tell users when they are basically sitting on their bitstream and mining it for information about their behavior is a good thing. The more interesting longer term prospect is going back to what is the nature of network effect market power? And if network effect market power is controlling a central database of you know, of all of this consumer activity, what would happen in a world where consumers had direct access to all the data that was being collected about them? You would essentially invert the market from one where we live, the one that we live in today, where consumers go to 20 or 30 different sites, each of which has an incomplete view of them, to one where the consumers create a data store that is a comprehensive data store of their behavior, and 20 or 30 different sites come to that data store on a permissioned basis. That fundamentally undermines network effect market power, and it would allow for the possibility of another wave of innovation. If you think about going back to the internet, why Tumblr was able to get to millions, 20 million users in seven or eight months. They didn't have to build the telecommunications network. They didn't have to acquire directly, you know, the capital costs of getting directly to consumers because we had this shared infrastructure. If we have an infrastructure where there's a decentralized data store where consumers have control over their data and are able to permission service providers to have access to that, You no longer need to get to the scale of a company like Facebook in order to be able to provide services to consumers. You can provide a very useful service to a consumer with a single permission from the consumer to have access to that comprehensive data store. So that is fundamentally transformative.

SPEAKER_5: That's a big idea. I agree. Somebody's going to make money off that. Of course, Jim Wright, shaking his head up there, knows that Larry Ellison is going to put that all in one data store that belonged to him because it's all in his customers'databases as it is right now. In other words, of course, if that doesn't happen, something else will happen. And the something else that will happen is the thing I spend a lot of time in classrooms trying to warn people about, which is the human race will be changed because the network becomes an agent and its so-called 'network effects'are how political power is distributed. You are, after all, proving what I hoped we could do, which is that anarchism and the venture capitalist community are, in fact, deeply allied here. The central idea is the re-decentralization of the web. Tim's idea is the central idea.think that I think that the the question is how does this technology about shareable forms of self-authenticating information help us to do that. I was reading an interesting paper by some computer scientists at Princeton earlier this week thinking about the use of blockchain like data storage to replace DNS, which, after all, is an awfully good idea of a very similar kind. It asks us to imagine decentralizing things that otherwise Poor Larry Strickling will have to go around lamenting that we cannot decentralize in any acceptable way for the remainder of the millennium, I think. We do have lots of ways in which this process of re-decentralization could occur through shareable self-authenticated information, which is blockchains, and whether that's in the supply chain or the way of making free software or the way of collecting consumer information. How the lawyers are going to think, this through in each of their little silos is certainly going to make a difference. In the sale of financial services, Keith, what Chris says is that you really do want to use stuff where the danger of fraud and abuse is present. It might not be a terribly bad idea to make sure that consumers actually could find out how many bank accounts they have at any particular bank and things like that, right? I mean, in the financial services industry, we have some reason for that in the same way that in the automotive industry, Jeremiah and I are going to talk about ways in which software cheating can ruin your whole company. As Mr. Stumpf has found out, I think that not keeping two sets of books is a good idea for a bank. What do I think is the way in which regulators are going to decide that they have a real benefit in that sense from increased integrity from this information?

SPEAKER_3: I think in some part it depends on what regulator you're talking about. Different regulators have different objectives, different goals. So your question would be probably more something like what the CFPB would be asking. The CFPB, their primary interest is consumer protection. They've pushed a lot of initiatives to make sure there's transparency for consumers. They understand what they're entering into. They understand whether they have one account or two accounts, what the payment terms are. They've really gone after people who have engaged in predatory practices like payday lenders and others. And so from their perspective, I think, They look at blockchain as the opportunity to increase transparency for individuals. You know, last week, Cordray is the head of the CFPB. He just gave a major speech about data aggregation, kind of who owns the data. Is it the financial institutions that owns the data or is it the customer that owns the data, which is exactly what you're talking about, right? And his view was it's the customer. It's the customer's data. So they should be able to take it with them. They should be able to pass the data on to, you know, other aggregators like Mint or Intuit, those kind of people. They should be able to have other people through APIs access that data. And so I think when you look at it from that perspective, right, they're kind of aligned with you. Take other regulators, take the OCC or the Fed. Again, their primary concern is safety and soundness of the system, not so much the individual but the system as a whole and how it helps function the economy. It's the Treasury Department that's declared financial infrastructure as part of the critical infrastructure of the United States. Their concern, using the consumer data as the example, is they care less about whether the consumer can access their data, who has it, where does it go. They're more concerned about if the data is going to leave, what are the consequences that go with it? So if you're letting data free flow in and out through APIs, for example, does that open up the bank to attack? So I think your question really depends on what perspective you're looking at.

SPEAKER_5: I'm also going to think if this is a systematically important institution that can be hit very hard by some sales incentives for its retail bankers out of control, then maybe it should also be the case the controller of currency cares about this at the level of how does bank examination really work and how good are we at testing the internal controls of big institutions before we have to lament that their internal controls weren't good enough by letting senators or something, right? I mean, there's payoff closer to the simple ground of making sure that safety and stability exists in the financial system too, if all that information is out there mineable in close to real time. Chris, I wanted to ask a question about slowing things down, which in your presentation is pure technological badness that we should get rid of by better algorithms. And yet I also recognize that one of the things which has happened in the trading system, in the financial trading system of the world is what Brad would call network effects of a different kind. Like I put my servers 200 nanoseconds closer to the exchange than the other fellow and I trade fast. When the length of time that a share of stock is held on the New York Stock Exchange converges or it's 22 seconds, maybe it wouldn't be such a bad idea if settlement speed went down in the technology of transacting. Is it possible that actually one of the things we get when we get shareable, self-authenticated information is that we return to better rather than worse timescales in some forms of trading? Is it always true that the faster clearing is the most important technological objective? As I was listening to you, I was thinking to myself, you know, it's an important enough idea nowadays that if you say, 'I'm going to have a new fully automated stock exchange and I'm going to have a speed bump in it,'people think that might be a selling point. What is the real? ACC thinks that. Yeah, well, so that is the real question in a way, which in a sense, Brad seems to me to be opening up again. Redecentralization also means decentralizing settlement in some important ways that might actually take valuable and salutary time and reduce flash crashes and other internet-enabled pathologies in the financial system.

SPEAKER_2: Yeah, so it's actually, there's an interesting dynamic going on, I think, because of the The disruptive nature of the technology and the potential for disintermediation that exists is that those like DTCC and CME and SWIFT and so forth are, if they're enlightened, they're having a very close look at this and trying to get their heads around it. And from my perspective, if they're smart, then they are. Looking at how they reinvent themselves, because they will be disintermediated eventually if they aren't an active participant in helping to sort of move things forward. Now, to your point, does it make sense to have speed bumps? And I think that's entirely possible, but again, it's going to depend on the nature of the type of use that you may want to bring this technology. In terms of slowing down, I think just in order to develop the software, and this is my problem as we're trying to do this, and I'm sure Ginny wouldn't want me to say this, but we need to slow down because we don't want to put stuff out that has the potential for 'oops.'I mean, that's just not cool. And so in the rush to getting software in place that we can use to do whatever it is, I think we need to be very deliberate and careful about this because, you know, if you put something in place that's intended to last for 20 years and you forgot to put in the provision for it, well, how do I update it, right? Oops, right? Or if you put in software that's got an inherent bug like the DAO and you rush to get something into production the way that they did without fixing it, you know, they're oops and you don't want those because.

SPEAKER_5: You want to talk about the investment risk of that?

No, I want to talk about that. You know that that problem I think that that it makes perfect sense that somebody in IBM or Bank of America's position would never want to put out a piece of code that could be hacked the way the DAO was hacked and that would be a huge brand risk. And I also think that one of the great opportunities for established institutions here is to be the backup version of trust in these systems.
