messages.content: The intent of SOPA is to rein in the offshore pirate sites that host a lot of copyrighted content. The challenge is this, that there is a regime called the Digital Millennium Copyright Act, or the DMCA. That creates a balance between the tech industry, the internet industry, and the content industry, largely Hollywood, record companies. That balance today is that if an internet company is made aware of copyrighted content on their site, they'll take it down. That's working pretty well domestically. The problem is that the DMCA doesn't reach offshore, and the Internet is a global phenomenon, and there's a bunch of sites in various parts of the world that are hosting intellectual property that was created in the United States and hosting it illegally. The content creators can't get at those sites because they're in jurisdictions where U .S.law has no effect. And so they can't get directly at the people who are breaking the law. So what they're trying to do is eliminate or sort of take those sites off of the U .S. Internet, make them invisible to U .S. users. In order to do that, they've asked Congress to make social networks, search engines, internet service providers, domain name service providers, payment processors and advertisers responsible for basically policing their users, basically preventing their users from seeing this content. So to make it sort of to give you an analogy that would make this a little bit more obvious. This is essentially making the phone company responsible for the illegal behavior of people using the phone network to commit crimes. The phone company is an intermediary. They're not committing the crimes. But if you made them responsible for any crimes that were committed on their network, you would certainly have an impact on crime. You would also clearly have an impact on the phone company. It would be a huge burden on the phone company. In the same way, This act places a huge burden on Internet services. A lot of services that are built on user-generated content like YouTube or Facebook are going to be responsible for policing those users. As you can imagine, they're very concerned about this new, brand new liability that this law would create for them. I have said in the past that essentially the Internet and the Internet culture is all about empowering the individual, empowering the edge, creating opportunity. And the content industry in this case is really trying to control. The behavior and those two philosophies are really in conflict. Let me give you a personal example. My wife and I have gotten into a series called Downton Abbey on the BBC, and a friend of ours alerted us to the series and we started watching it, and it's a great sort of upstairs downstairs story, and it was very well done, and we went through the whole first season on Netflix in about two weeks. Our friend says, you know, the second season is available too, and we go, great, let's go watch the second season. So we went to watch it, and then we get pointed to this site, I think it was called Mega Upload. And I'm starting to think, oh, I'm not sure I want to do this. I'm in the middle of this debate. I don't want to go someplace that may not be legal. I said, I don't like the sound of this. Let me go see if I can find this content legitimately. And so I go to the BBC website in Europe. And sure enough, the entire second season, every episode is available for streaming from the BBC site in London. So I launch episode one. And the first thing that happens is a little dialogue box comes up and says, oh, you're not in the United Kingdom. You're in the United States. You can't watch this. What that made me realize is that there's a whole lot of piracy that's going on as a result of the fact that you just can't get it. I mean, I would have been happy to pay. I pay Netflix for the content. I would have been happy to pay. So it's not as much about whether this is right or wrong. The problem is that the industry grew up in an era where distribution was very expensive and production was very expensive. And so they parceled out rights very carefully, rights to windows in time, release windows in various different media, and then also geographic windows. And the rights are all very convoluted and complicated. And we now live in a world where everything is accessible globally on the internet. And they're struggling to adapt to that. And they're trying to prevent the distribution of content across this network because it doesn't conform with the way they've been licensing content over time. We think that they're going to need to ultimately adapt to it because it doesn't really make sense to criminalize all of your most passionate fans. You've got to find a way to serve them in the medium that allows for access to this content very inexpensively. And I think they can. I think they will.

Bitcoin. Because oftentimes there are, there's founding members and it becomes unevenly distributed sometimes. And you come up with the governance problems as they arise because people are incentivized to keep. And if we don't open that up, we are not going to be autonomous actors working against information that is, you know, that we're choosing and algorithms that we're choosing. So that's my whole.

So I think that is super important even as we're talking now and thinking about that a lot. And then the other thing would be I'm forgetting it now. So the identity part, right? So that's obviously super important that now we're centralizing our data with ourselves. And I think that's the question that people need to be thinking about, and then also saying: So what would that identity governance layer be from the beginning before we get into this? I think what we realize with the Internet is like these things can't really be afterthoughts. They're not that effective. Yeah.

SPEAKER_4: Can I just plug something linked to that? Because we've just invested in a company called Evinim, that's open-sourced. That's all identity is. And they allow for self-sovereignty in a decentralized structure. So it already exists. Maybe it's not the one. I think it is. But I think everything starts with that identity layer and self-sovereignty. From that base, you build everything up. Sorry.

SPEAKER_1: No, I think that's, in terms of what you can do today, that's a wonderful vision. I think you're absolutely right that identity is a very complex issue. Down to my 14-year-old son. Just the other day, we were standing in line going to Ethereum DevCon, and it was a long line, and so he wanted to pass the time. So, of course, he's talking to me about games. And he makes this astounding proposal. So, the proposal that he makes is that the metagame, the evolution of the game for baseball, basketball, football, is nothing like the metagame for esports. He's really into gaming. He loves the fact that Smash Brothers Melee is moving many, many times faster than you see in these older traditional games. And I was astounded by, first of all, the fact that a 14-year-old boy is making these kinds of observations. And I want to go back to play off of your Because what I've heard in this room is designing markets. What I've heard is reclaiming our notion of identity and our notion and redecentralizing the web. So the metagame here, you talked over, the word you used again and again and again was evolutionary, right? The metagame here is super, super fast. And the reason this is important, so now to bring it very, very concrete, is because if you look at it, there's not one way to coordinate each other. We have finance as one coordination technology. So markets are one clear, obvious coordination technology. Governance is another coordination technology. We've mentioned Facebook several times. Social media has emerged as yet a third form of coordination technology. None. These coordination technologies currently are moving fast enough for us to organize ourselves with respect to the consequences of how we have lived on planet Earth. Climate change is moving much faster than we think it is and we have to learn to coordinate ourselves very fast. So this exciting new technology is very practically showing up at a time when we need to up our metagame. And so that's why it's important not just to say what can it do for us now, but what can it do for us in relation to what's really the existential threat of the present moment.

For me, it was just like this raw pattern recognition, like, oh, this very reminiscent of something that was big, big, big and influential in my life. And this feels like it's going to happen all over again. Like everything at USV, I came in with a ton of energy for this thing. And Brad and Albert and everybody else, it sounds like you were just arriving at that time. It ended up in a very long conversation and we debated and discussed it. And ultimately, Albert had some important insights to contribute around this being a protocol, which I think unlocked a lot of things for us as we thought about how to invest in it. SPEAKER_4: Okay. So, you know, there's a lot of competition on the market, and you're speaking about open sourcing, right? So basically we're speaking about a lot of companies who are facilitators of aggregating offer and demand. Offer and demand, well, demands and yeah, I'm right. And so you have, you were speaking about data as well. And so you have examples like Snowden, you know, consulting for WhatsApp for their Android app. And you have Ello, that's kind of a.like a social network, but a little bit really secure. Not secure, but you know it. And so your job, you have a commission, you're saying, and these companies that have power from data, they're losing it. And you were saying there's this shift. How do you see that these companies that do lose this power can exist? Did I make sense?

SPEAKER_1: Well, I think the companies that are likely to lose the power over time are the incumbents, the Facebooks, the Googles of the world. And I think it will happen slowly. I don't think it will happen quickly. I mean, I don't think that there's going to be some watershed moment in the middle of 2015 where all of a sudden we're going to see their dominance start to wane. And I think it'll probably, you'll start to see it when you see new services emerge that people really adopt and start using. And many of the companies that you mentioned or open source projects you mentioned actually haven't been successful. So maybe they weren't architected properly. Maybe they're not solving a problem that people care about yet. Sometimes timing is important. When the right product comes to the market at the right time. We may be preaching about a moment that's five or ten years away.

So one of the things that we've done as a firm, and Fred has been obviously the person that's created the most here, is we've been very open about all of our ideas. And we have these conversations amongst ourselves. But then everybody in the room, with me as the possible exception, goes out and blogs about those conversations that we're having in our partners'meetings. And our limited partners come to us and say, 'What the hell are you doing?'That's a proprietary asset that you guys control, and you're putting it out there in the market for everybody. And we think that what happens as a result is we get into conversations like this, and we get into conversations with the smart people who aren't in the room, and there are always more smart people outside the room than there are inside the room. So that's all by way of saying that there was an assumption embedded in your question that you have to control something in order to be able to exploit it or make profit from it. I would rather think about it in terms of being at the front end of the flow. If you can just be in the flow and you can be exposed to ideas more quickly as a company or as a venture firm or as an individual, you will do better. And it doesn't mean that you have to control it.

SPEAKER_5: Hi, I have a question. Two questions, actually. First is, London or Berlin? It's just a clear question. And then the second question is, you obviously made an investment in one football app recently, or about a year ago, two years ago. And you've made a number of investments in London, Halo. Can you talk a little bit about Berlin? So we're in Berlin, and a lot of the companies here are thinking about investment opportunities. Talk a little bit about what perhaps you're looking for, and yeah. What the opportunities are for potential Berlin startups that might want an investment from USV?

SPEAKER_1: Well, I hesitate to say London versus Berlin because we invest in both cities. But what I will say is I think they're very different cities. I think that in Berlin, you have what I feel like is a more sort of grassroots, gritty kind of tech-heavy, lower-cost, leaner kind of mindset about starting and building companies. Berlin's a lot less expensive to live in. It's got a younger, I feel, highly creative talent pool here. London is a more established market.probably more executive talent there, but it's a very expensive place to live. And for certain kinds of things, financial services, for example, maybe media, London would be a more logical place to start a company because of all of the talent that exists in those industries. But if you were going to build something that was kind of a new kind of business that didn't exist before, Berlin might be a great place to do that. So I think they're different.but both have strengths and weaknesses, and we, like I said, are happy to invest in both markets.

I think there's one thing that Berlin could use more of, and that is young, technically savvy venture capitalists and who control their own deals. So I have a sense that there's a number of firms that are European firms. They tend not to be headquartered in Berlin, but they have a couple of young scouts in Berlin. But those scouts take these really interesting deals back to a firm that's saying, well, where's the revenue? And it's hard to get those deals done. And I would just encourage all of you to encourage anybody who's in that position to to get their own fund off the ground so that you can have some local venture capital run by young people who understand the technology that you guys are building.

SPEAKER_8: Hey guys, lots of interesting stuff about the future, but looking back, what was the investment that you missed that you wish you hadn't?

SPEAKER_1: Well, I wrote about this. There's lots of them, by the way. I could go on for hours about this. The one I wrote about was Airbnb, and the reason I wrote about it was that the reason we missed it, to me, was very instructive, and I wanted to share it with people. We could not imagine that they could move upmarket the way they did. When we met with them, it was literally people sleeping on air mattresses in people's living rooms, and that's what it was. Didn't want to invest in that, we didn't understand that what they were really building was a marketplace that would ultimately move to Bedrooms and ultimately move to full homes, and that it could in time, you know, effectively replace staying in hotels for many people, so we missed that one.

We made one other mistake there, which was that I at least put myself in the position of sleeping on that air mattress and said, 'I'm not going to do this. Why is anybody going to do this?'And that's a really, really common venture capital mistake to think that you're the customer and to put yourself in the position of being the customer. Try not to make that mistake again.

SPEAKER_3: All right. I think one or two more questions. Unless you guys feel like you want to go on forever, well, I'd rather work the crowd a little bit, you know. So final question, anybody? And then Fred and Brad will stick around so you can obviously approach these guys. They'll be here for some more time.

SPEAKER_7: I would like to know as an entrepreneur starting that early and being so innovative, I would like to know what do you think of AI and are you someone investing in that?

SPEAKER_1: I would like to think that we, as Brad said earlier, we don't like to invest in technologies. We like to invest in things that are enabled by technology. So I think we would be very excited about investing in a business that was making something possible that everybody would want, and using AI as a tool or the tool to make that happen. But if someone came to us and said, 'I built the next great AI engine', I don't think we'd invest in that.

SPEAKER_3: All right, this last question. Hi.

SPEAKER_2: Last question, right here? Okay. A friend of mine founded a satellite company, and he also, together with that, created an ethics committee. And he went online to try and research other companies that had ethics committees to use that as reference, and couldn't find any. And so what do you think the place of ethics is in business? And, you know, where do VCs, you know, where should VCs'place be in that? Because we've found as companies grow, many of them, you know, it turns out they've done very questionable practices which harm their employees'rights and individual freedoms and so forth. And so, yeah, that's it.

So I do have an opinion on this, which is, and this is another debate that we have internally. This is again a debate that I have with Albert. He's a very big fan of something called a benefit corp, which at least begins to get at the idea that the corporation's job isn't just to create money for shareholders. It also has a second set of responsibilities to customers, employees, environment, whatever. And so he is really passionate about seeing that model in the marketplace. I look at it and think, you know, It's an information problem, right? I mean, when you mention that we find out later that a company has done questionable things, somehow that information wasn't available broadly. And if you think about a lot of the stuff that we do, we're investing in these networks where the company themselves creates nothing. It's the creator and the consumer, the driver and passenger. Those people are attributing their own personal brand to an affiliation of some kind with some network, right? And if that network is evil, good people aren't going to affiliate with that network, right? So I think that there's an; I think capitalism is actually a pretty fantastic system. And I think that what's happening now is, as we move into this network economy, is that capitalism is evolving in such a way that you can't get away with that shit. That you can't be that way and expect somebody to come onto the network and create value for you. If you're not actually living the values that you are espousing and sort of creating in your network, you're not gonna last. So I think that; I don't know that you need to create an ethics committee. I think that actually feels a little bit artificial and I don't think you necessarily need to brand yourself as a benefit corp. I think you need to be upfront about your values and you need to implement your values in your systems and through the way the architecture of the system works and you then need to live them.

SPEAKER_1: The only thing I would add to that is that the actions of individual employees of a company don't necessarily reflect the values of the company, but if the company tolerates those actions, then they become complicit in that, right? So, if the employee of one of our companies did something badly, and they were terminated, it doesn’t mean that our company acted badly, it means an employee acted badly. But if they did it and it was public and they were allowed to stay employed, and then another person did it and they were allowed to stay employed, then that company has effectively become complicit in that. So it’s very important as a CEO of a company, it's a very hard thing to do, to police your companies and demand that employees live up to your value system. And if they don’t live up to your value system, that they leave your company. And where it's really painful is when an employee makes a mistake. And they've been a good employee for years and years and years, and they do something stupid, like say something stupid in public on Twitter or something like that, and you have to fire them. And you know they're a good person. You know they just did something stupid. Maybe they were drunk or whatever. Like Uber, no? Well, I'm not talking about Uber. As a CEO, these are very hard decisions you have to make. And a lot of CEOs don't want to make them because they think that they're being cruel or harsh. But the problem is that if you're not willing to enforce your values as a CEO of a company, then the values are weakened. And it's easy for me to say, because I'm not a CEO of any company. Brad and I and a few other people run USV, and we haven't faced this issue, thankfully, inside USV, but I have been on many boards where CEOs face this issue, and it's a very hard thing to actually live up to your values in the moment.

SPEAKER_2: Do you think it's necessary to fire those people when they make that one mistake?

SPEAKER_1: Well, I mean, that's the question, right? That's the hard question, right? If I don't, then does everybody else in this company believe that it is then okay to behave that way? And then, do you in effect endorse that behavior? This is where being a CEO gets hard. This is one of many places where being a CEO gets hard. Brad, Fred, thank you so much for sharing your knowledge with us here today.

SPEAKER_3: We have two little presents for you. I don't know if you're foodies. But basically, those are Stollen. It's a traditional sort of Christmas cake. It's full of gluten, full of dairy, full of sugar. And weighs a lot. And weighs a lot.

messages.content: SPEAKER_1: Now to conclude this morning's segment, I'd like to welcome from New York City, Managing Director of Union Square Ventures, Brad Burnham. Brad, welcome to Chattanooga.

So it's going to be hard to summon that kind of passion, but I respect it and appreciate it, and I think it's an incredibly important. I was going to talk a little bit about the possibilities, but I think Jonathan did a wonderful job of illustrating the possibilities of of gigabit fiber. I'm going to start by telling you a little bit about who I am and what we do, and that may give you a little bit of context. I actually started my career in 1979 with AT&T prior to divestiture and then lived through divestiture, so I had this kind of funny regulatory experience there. But I spent the last 20 years investing in the applications layer, mostly of the internet. And first it was AT&T Ventures and then ultimately at Union Square Ventures in New York. And I learned a couple of different things from both of those experiences. The first part of it was a real appreciation for the service ethic that existed at AT&T way back in the 80s, 70s really. And their real commitment to delivering a platform that people could communicate on and their sense of public duty around that. And I really learned to respect that level of commitment. When I got involved in the venture capital business and began investing in the applications layer of the web, I really understood the difference between that service ethic and an innovation ethic. And what happens on the internet is really phenomenal. It's driven by the lack of permission. The internet, and maybe we're stepping back and talking about it for a second, was a complete accident. If AT&T had not been deregulated, we would not have the internet. The first thing was the Carterphone decision, which led to the creation of modems, which led to dial-up ISPs, which made it possible for people to access this thing that was, at the time, just a university network. But when they accessed it, because they accessed it through dial-up modems, because there were a bunch of tiny little businesses that had these modem banks that connected people to the internet, all of which were very competitive, there was no permission required to get online. And there was no permission required to put a website up online. And as a result, we had this enormous profusion of innovation that happened, because you didn't have to ask. And so I had in my own life, very stark examples of the difference between a permissioned environment and a permissionless environment. You know, we are investors at AT&T Ventures in things like Twitter and Tumblr and Foursquare and Etsy and all of these businesses. If you had to ask somebody, even if you had to ask a boss, does this make sense? The answer would be no. It doesn't make sense. It doesn't fit with our strategy. It potentially compromises our existing business model. We don't know that there's enough customers for it. You can't prove that there are customers for it. We're not going to do it. Because they had to ask no permission, it was able to get up there instantly. I also, early in my career, when I was at AT&T Ventures, experienced a little bit of what it was like to invest in a permissioned environment. I was an investor in a company called Classic Sports Network, which was old sports. Crazy idea. Sports that had already been played. But it turned out to be a pretty neat business, and it grew very rapidly. But at the time, there were about 38 channels on most cable networks. And for classic sports to get a channel on a cable network is very, very, very difficult. Ultimately, we negotiated a deal that essentially gave up 18% of the company to John Malone in order to get onto a network. That's a permissioned environment. If each of these internet startups had to give up equity to Comcast in order to get distributed, none of them would get funded, right? So that's the problem. So here's the concern I have. We had this phenomenal profusion of innovation as a result of this permissionless environment. It is going away. We don't all appreciate why it existed. It existed because we started out in a dial-up environment, which was competitive, and nobody messed with the content because, first of all, they didn't know how to, and second of all, they were in a competitive environment. Then when we moved from dial-up to broadband, we very quickly narrowed the range of choices. You had two choices. You had a DSL choice and a cable modem choice. As the demand for more speed continued to grow, the DSL choice became less and less viable, and we really ended up with a single choice for what I might think of as advanced broadband in most markets, and that's a cable modem. Well, the cable companies came from a completely different place. They came from a world where they had a great deal of control over what was actually done on their network, and they were completely vertically integrated. So all of the content was delivered, all of the user experience was controlled. And when they arrived on the scene and all of a sudden became the largest distributor of broadband, they went to the FCC and they said, you know, we want to do more of this, but we don't want to be considered an essential service. We want to be considered an information service. And so they got the FCC in 2003 or 2002, I think, actually, originally, to agree that they could continue to deploy broadband, but they didn't have to be regulated as an essential telecommunications service. And so that permissionless environment that we had for so long is beginning to be eroded. And now we're beginning to see the stuff that Jonathan talked about, you know, the relationship between Comcast and Netflix. And, you know, Comcast is saying, well, gosh, you know, these guys are dumping a ton of bits on our network. And, you know, we will peer with people, you know, we'll share, you know, we'll exchange data with people when that exchange is symmetrical. But these guys are just dumping all the stuff and we're not sending anything to Netflix. Well, of course you're not sending anything to Netflix because you are what's called a terminating access monopoly. You are the last mile that goes to the customers. And because the entire history of media in this country has been broadcast, you've got a large volume of stuff coming downstream. The only way that Netflix can get to a customer on a Comcast network is through Comcast. And they're beginning to say, well, gosh, that's a pretty highly leverageable position. We probably should take a piece of that transaction. Well, that is fundamentally new on the internet. There has never been a notion that content providers had to pay. There was a notion that you could put your server up on the net and anybody could reach it. And the only content that's being delivered to a Netflix subscriber is content that that subscriber asked for. That access provider committed to provide access to the internet. But now they're trying to change the definition of the internet. So why is all that important? Well, we've been very actively involved in a conversation that you've all heard about, about network neutrality. And, you know, the FCC is currently considering a set of rules that would define what defines neutrality. What, you know, that last mile, it really is, you know, think of it as not a duopoly, but think of it as an advanced broadband case, a monopoly in most places. And in all cases, a terminating access monopoly, because all of the customers behind one network or another, and there's only one way to reach them. That leverageable position has a certain set of responsibilities to behave in a neutral way. So that's what's being debated right now. Well, in one way, that's a really important discussion. And frankly, we believe that this is an essential. I've heard a lot of conversation earlier today about communications being an essential, almost human right. It's certainly an essential service. And we think.that being that provider of that communication is a significant responsibility. And we think in order to protect innovation on the internet, it's important that that be treated as neutral. There's a bunch of tricky problems associated with implementing that. You're asking a federal regulatory agency to come in and decide exactly how it should be implemented. If you want to be entertained, it's not all that entertaining, but go back and read some of the rulings in 1996, 2000, about why certain FCC regulations were made. And you'll realize that the FCC had no idea what was coming. And so the problem with regulatory solutions is that they have to be made in the context of what you know today, and you can't anticipate the future. So the better solution is to create more competition. Of course, the same people who are opposing any kind of regulation of their terminating access monopolies are also saying, well, we don't think competition is a good idea. Well, what we're talking about here today is a model that is more resilient, more flexible, more dynamic, and it's a model that brings competition to this important last mile. It brings connectivity to the first place to places where there is no competition and there's nobody pulling fiber or pulling any kind of high-speed internet access. So I think that we all need to be supporting this notion of opening up these markets, increasing competition, narrowing that portion of the market, that segment of the market, which is this, by definition, kind of a natural monopoly, allowing for lots of innovation upstream in the areas of CDNs, those are content delivery networks, or hosting services, backbone broadband access services, and then allowing for lots of innovation and competition on top of those services. And the way we can do that is by, you know, yes, we should move forward with network neutrality rules, and at the same time, we should be very, very actively promoting competition. And I appreciate all the work that people here are doing in that way, or to that end. Thank you.
